perturbation.original_text: 1.9 The Sub-Advisor shall act honestly, with due skill, care and diligence and fairly and in the best interest of the Partnership in carrying out its obligations under this Agreement and shall use all reasonable endeavours to perform its obligations under this Agreement in accordance with FSMA, the FCA Rules and any other laws, regulations, guidelines and guidance as may be in force from time to time and applicable to the Funds and their business or to the Sub-Advisor ("Applicable Law").
perturbation.changed_text: 1.9 The Sub-Advisor shall endeavor to act in what it perceives to be the best interest of the Partnership when carrying out its obligations under this Agreement and shall attempt to perform its obligations in general accordance with Applicable Law.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall act honestly, with due skill, care and diligence and fairly and in the best interest' with 'shall endeavor to act in what it perceives to be the best interest' introduces subjectivity and weakens the standard of care. Similarly, replacing 'shall use all reasonable endeavors to perform its obligations under this Agreement in accordance with' with 'shall attempt to perform its obligations in general accordance with' weakens the commitment to compliance with Applicable Law. This is an attempt to introduce ambiguity in the professional standard that is expected of the Sub-Advisor, moving away from the legal standard.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 206 of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 prohibits investment advisors from engaging in fraudulent, deceptive, or manipulative practices. The modified text weakens the fiduciary duty of the Sub-Advisor by making their obligations subjective and less stringent, potentially conflicting with the requirement to act in the client's best interest with reasonable care and diligence. Specifically, changing ‘shall act honestly, with due skill, care and diligence’ to ‘shall endeavor to act in what it perceives to be the best interest’ and changing 'shall use all reasonable endeavors to perform its obligations under this Agreement in accordance with Applicable Law' to 'shall attempt to perform its obligations in general accordance with Applicable Law' creates a loophole where the Sub-Advisor's subjective perception and a general attempt at compliance may not meet the legal threshold of acting in the client's best interest, violating Section 206.
perturbation.justification: The original contract requires the Sub-Advisor to 'act honestly, with due skill, care and diligence and fairly and in the best interest of the Partnership', while the changed text states the Sub-Advisor 'shall endeavor to act in what it perceives to be the best interest'. This introduces subjectivity and weakens the commitment. Similarly, the original text mandates using 'all reasonable endeavors to perform its obligations...in accordance with Applicable Law,' but the changed text uses 'shall attempt to perform its obligations in general accordance with Applicable Law,' weakening the commitment to compliance. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both clearly state this change weakens the fiduciary duty of the Sub-Advisor, potentially conflicting with acting in the client's best interest. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references sections of the Investment Advisers Act, including 15 U.S.C. 80b-6, which supports the relevance of the legal principle cited in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: 6.1 The Sub-Advisor shall maintain proper and complete records relating to the services to be provided under this Agreement for such period of time as may be required under Applicable Law, including (as applicable, in respect of the relevant Discretionary Funds) records with respect to the acquisition, holding and disposal of securities on behalf of the Funds, details of all brokers used and the aggregate dollar amount of brokerage commission paid in that regard to each broker.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1 The Sub-Advisor should maintain records that it deems appropriate relating to the services to be provided under this Agreement, generally following what it understands to be the expectations of Applicable Law.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies that the Sub-Advisor 'shall maintain proper and complete records...for such period of time as may be required under Applicable Law'. The modified version replaces this with 'should maintain records that it deems appropriate relating to the services to be provided under this Agreement, generally following what it understands to be the expectations of Applicable Law'. This weakens the obligation to maintain proper and complete records for a period required by law, and introduces a subjective element where the Sub-Advisor determines what is appropriate and what they understand as the expectations of law. This creates ambiguity and allows for potential non-compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 204-2 under the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 details specific requirements for recordkeeping by investment advisers. The modified text, which allows the Sub-Advisor to maintain records 'that it deems appropriate' and 'generally following what it understands to be the expectations of Applicable Law,' contradicts this rule. Rule 204-2 requires specific records to be kept (e.g., journals, ledgers, memoranda of orders, etc.) for a defined period. The modified text introduces ambiguity and discretion that could result in the Sub-Advisor not maintaining all records required by Rule 204-2 or not retaining them for the required period, thereby violating the rule.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 275.204-2 - Books and records to be maintained by investment advisers. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 275.204-2 - Books and records to be maintained by investment advisers. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 275.204-2 Books and records to be maintained by investment advisers. (a) Every investment adviser registered or required to be registered under section 203 of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 80b-3 ) shall make and keep true, accurate and current the following books and records relating to its investment advisory business; (1) A journal or journals, including cash receipts and disbursements, records, and any other records of original entry forming the basis of entries in any ledger. (2) General and auxiliary ledgers (or other comparable records) reflecting asset, liability, reserve, capital, income and expense accounts. (3) A memorandum of each order given by the investment adviser for the purchase or sale of any security, of any instruction received by the investment adviser concerning the purchase, sale, receipt or delivery of a particular security, and of any modification or cancellation of any such order or instruction. Such memoranda shall show the terms and conditions of the order, instruction, modification or cancellation; shall identify the person connected with the investment adviser who recommended the transaction to the client and the person who placed such order; and shall show the account for which entered, the date of entry, and the bank, broker or dealer by or through whom executed where appropriate. Orders entered pursuant to the exercise of discretionary power shall be so designated. (4) All check books, bank statements, cancelled checks and cash reconciliations of the investment adviser. (5) All bills or statements (or copies thereof), paid or unpaid, relating to the business of the investment adviser as such. (6) All trial balances, financ
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts Rule 204-2 under the Investment Advisers Act of 1940, which mandates specific recordkeeping requirements for investment advisers. The original text states the Sub-Advisor 'shall maintain proper and complete records...for such period of time as may be required under Applicable Law', creating a clear obligation. The modified text weakens this, stating the Sub-Advisor 'should maintain records that it deems appropriate...generally following what it understands to be the expectations of Applicable Law.' This introduces ambiguity and discretion. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 17 CFR § 275.204-2, explicitly states that investment advisers 'shall make and keep true, accurate and current the following books and records relating to its investment advisory business,' listing specific requirements like journals, ledgers, and memoranda of orders. The modified text's subjective 'deems appropriate' and 'understands to be the expectations' directly undermines the specific and mandatory nature of Rule 204-2, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 7.1 In providing its services under this Agreement, the Sub-Advisor will discharge its duties in accordance with the same standard of care established for Oaktree US in the relevant Fund Agreements, and will be indemnified by each of the Funds as an agent of Oaktree US in accordance with such Fund Agreements. To the extent Oaktree US and its affiliates, directors, officers, employees, shareholders, assigns, representatives or agents (apart from the Sub-Advisor) (collectively, "Oaktree US Indemnities") suffer any liability, loss (including amounts paid in settlement), damages or expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees) (collectively "Losses") in connection with the Funds, and:- (a) Oaktree US Indemnities are not indemnified by the Funds for such Losses under the indemnification provisions of the applicable Fund Agreements; (b) such Losses were suffered by virtue of the Sub-Advisor's or its employees' acts or omissions, or alleged acts or omissions under this Agreement; and (c) the Sub-Advisor (including its employees) is guilty of negligence or wilful misconduct, then the Sub-Advisor will hold Oaktree US Indemnities harmless and indemnify it for such Losses; provided that the Sub-Advisor shall not be liable for actions or omissions to act ordered by Oaktree US to which the Sub-Advisor objected in writing at the time of such order.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.1 In providing its services under this Agreement, the Sub-Advisor will endeavor to generally meet the standard of care established for Oaktree US in the relevant Fund Agreements. To the extent Oaktree US and its affiliates, directors, officers, employees, shareholders, assigns, representatives or agents (apart from the Sub-Advisor) (collectively, "Oaktree US Indemnities") suffer any liability, loss (including amounts paid in settlement), damages or expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees) (collectively "Losses") in connection with the Funds, and: (a) Oaktree US Indemnities are not indemnified by the Funds for such Losses under the indemnification provisions of the applicable Fund Agreements; (b) such Losses were allegedly suffered by virtue of the Sub-Advisor's or its employees' acts or omissions under this Agreement; and (c) the Sub-Advisor (including its employees) is potentially found to be indirectly connected to negligence, then the Sub-Advisor may consider holding Oaktree US Indemnities harmless for such Losses.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause clearly states that the Sub-Advisor 'will discharge its duties in accordance with the same standard of care...' and 'will hold Oaktree US Indemnities harmless and indemnify it for such Losses' if specific conditions are met. The modification introduces significant ambiguity by replacing 'will discharge its duties' with 'will endeavor to generally meet the standard of care,' weakening the commitment. Condition (b) also has been altered to 'such Losses were allegedly suffered by virtue of the Sub-Advisor's or its employees' acts or omissions under this Agreement', removing the definitive statement and instead using an alleged one, to further weaken the obligation. Replacing 'is guilty of negligence or wilful misconduct' with 'is potentially found to be indirectly connected to negligence' introduces uncertainty, and replacing 'will hold Oaktree US Indemnities harmless and indemnify it for such Losses' with 'may consider holding Oaktree US Indemnities harmless for such Losses' makes the indemnification discretionary rather than mandatory. This significantly weakens the legal obligation and introduces multiple points of ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 206 of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 prohibits investment advisors from engaging in fraudulent, deceptive, or manipulative practices. By weakening the standard of care and making indemnification discretionary, the modified text potentially allows the Sub-Advisor to avoid liability for actions that would constitute a breach of fiduciary duty under the Act. Specifically, the introduction of subjectivity into the standard of care ('endeavor to generally meet') and the uncertainty around indemnification ('may consider holding harmless') creates a situation where the Sub-Advisor might not be held accountable for conduct that harms the client, thus violating Section 206's prohibition of deceptive or manipulative practices.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that weaken the legal obligations of the Sub-Advisor. The original contract states the Sub-Advisor 'will discharge its duties in accordance with the same standard of care...', creating a definitive obligation. The changed text uses 'will endeavor to generally meet the standard of care,' which weakens this commitment. This introduces subjectivity and makes it difficult to enforce a specific standard of care. Also, the original text says the Sub-Advisor 'will hold Oaktree US Indemnities harmless and indemnify it for such Losses', but is changed to 'may consider holding Oaktree US Indemnities harmless for such Losses', making indemnification discretionary rather than mandatory. This undermines the original obligation and creates a legal contradiction because the definite requirement of indemnification has been removed. Law Snippet 1 lists several regulations that relate to investment advisors, including sections of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940, which supports the notion that obligations of advisors must be clear and not ambiguous.
